old_claim.note: Loom provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The source-backed sail form is a square canvas or textile surface; textile production enables sail material without requiring the specific loom node as a direct prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Sailing ships / Types of sails: describes early sailing ships using a large square canvas suspended from a yard, and says the spread of canvas in sails determined speed.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes sail propulsion through square canvas surfaces suspended from yards and carried on masts.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports textile production as the relevant material capability while rejecting loom as an over-specific direct prerequisite for the corrected early-sailing scope.

ontology_before: Sailing depended directly on loom, implying that a specific loom technology was required for all source-backed early sailing.
ontology_after: Sailing depends on textiles_weaving as the broader sail-material capability, while loom remains a separate textile-production technology.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: sailing now depends on textiles_weaving and no longer depends directly on loom.
would_reject_if: The sailing node is rescoped to a specific loom-made sail tradition rather than early sail propulsion.
A source shows the Naqada II ship-under-sail evidence required loom technology rather than broader textile production.
The textiles_weaving node is narrowed away from cloth or canvas production.
short_reason: Textile sail material is the supported prerequisite; loom was too specific for the corrected scope.
